name: Spermidine improved vaccine-response markers in older non-responders
summary: A small older-adult pilot adds a human immune-function signal for spermidine, concentrated in baseline vaccine non-responders.
statement: In a double-blind randomized pilot in adults over 65, 13 weeks of 6 mg/day spermidine was associated with improved SARS-CoV-2 vaccine-response markers in the baseline vaccine non-responder subgroup, including higher spike-specific IgG, memory B-cell recall responses, and neutralizing antibody activity.
population_or_model: Adults over 65 years of age after a third SARS-CoV-2 vaccine dose
time_horizon: 13 weeks of supplementation with follow-up to 37 weeks
quantitative_note: In the eight spermidine-arm vaccine non-responders, mean anti-spike IgG rose from 377.42 to 28,331.02 by week 2; whole-arm anti-spike IgG increases were not statistically significant.
caveats: The trial was small, exploratory, and not formally powered for definitive efficacy.
The main positive signal was concentrated in an imbalanced baseline non-responder subgroup rather than the whole spermidine arm.
The endpoint was vaccine-response immunology, not clinical infection prevention, frailty, mortality, or broad healthy-aging function.
Vaccination schedules, treatment timing, and dietary spermidine intake were not fully controlled.
